data.title: New tariff rates mean even more revenue for U.S. Treasury
data.description:
The U.S. Treasury is set to see another big cash windfall after fresh tariffs were imposed on much of the globe following an August 1 deadline. Even countries that have reached new trade understandings with the United States have still had to agree to what was once considered steep tariff rates, like the 15% levies imposed on Japan and the European Union. Markets have taken a dip on this new reality, but stocks are still near record highs and have not yet displayed the panic seen in the aftermath of "Liberation Day" in April. Plans for Canada and pause for Mexico

Snapshot: Tariffs are paid to the U.S. government by importers, who look to manage the duties in several ways. That includes raising retail prices, which has sparked fears of inflation, but can also be addressed via other methods like discounts, alternative sourcing, lower profit margins, or cutting company costs. The impacts of each have been ferociously debated, but the one thing that is clear is that tariffs are raising a tremendous amount of money for Uncle Sam.

Besides filling U.S. coffers to pay down debt, Trump is seeking to utilize tariffs as a way to spark a manufacturing renaissance in which America once again becomes an industrial powerhouse. The question is whether much of that can come back to the U.S., and how long it would take. Seeking Alpha subscribers are as polarized as politics on this topic, with a clear 50-50 split on Trump's trade strategy (note that this was one of the biggest surveys by Wall Street Breakfast, with over 4,000 responses!).

Show me the money: Last month, the U.S. Treasury posted a rare $27B June budget surplus for the first time in nearly a decade. It was helped by tariff-related revenues and customs duties, which Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent expects to "be well over $300B by the end of the year." However, the U.S. is still running a fiscal year-to-date deficit of $1.34T, meaning action will also have to be taken on the spending side of the equation in order to achieve a balanced budget.

data.title: Trump administration to trial Medicare, Medicaid coverage for obesity drugs: WaPO
data.description:
Obesity drugmakers Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) and Novo Nordisk (NVO) traded higher in the premarket on Friday after The Washington Post reported that the Trump Administration plans to cover weight loss drugs for some Medicare and Medicaid enrollees under a five-year experimental program.

Citing a document obtained from the U.S. Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, the state Medicaid programs and Medicare Part D plans could voluntarily offer coverage for Novo’s (NVO) Ozempic and Wegovy and Eli Lilly’s (NYSE:LLY) Mounjaro and Zepbound.

The experiment comes at a time of mounting affordability concerns surrounding their highly popular GLP-1 drugs, indicated for both diabetes and weight loss.

The program, conducted through a testing lab called the Center for Medicare and Medicaid Innovation (CMMI) is expected to begin in April 2026 for Medicaid and January 2027 for Medicare plans.

When asked about comments, a CMS spokesperson said, “All drug coverages undergo a cost-benefit review. CMS does not comment on potential models or coverage.”

The proposal isn’t finalized, and the CMS can—but is not required to—subject it to a formal process where it seeks public comments about the program before finalizing it.

data.title: TransAlta GAAP EPS of -C$0.38, revenue of C$433M
data.description:
• TransAlta press release (NYSE:TAC): Q2 GAAP EPS of -C$0.38.
  • Revenue of C$433M (-25.6% Y/Y).
  • Adjusted EBITDA of C$349 million, compared to C$316 million for the same period in 2024.
  • Free Cash Flow of C$177 million, or C$0.60 per share, remained consistent with the same period in 2024.
  • Cash flow from operating activities of C$157 million, or C$0.53 per share, compared to C$108 million, or C$0.36 per share, from the same period in 2024.

data.title: Chevron in charts: Q2 U.S. upstream production growth rate improves Y/Y
Moderna beats top-line and bottom-line estimates; updates FY25 outlook
data.description:
• Moderna press release (NASDAQ:MRNA): Q2 GAAP EPS of -$2.13 beats by $0.84.
  • Revenue of $142M (-41.1% Y/Y) beats by $29.44M.

  • Cash, cash equivalents and investments as of June 30, 2025, were $7.5 billion, compared to $8.4 billion as of March 31, 2025. The decrease during the quarter was primarily due to ongoing research and development expenses and other operating activities.

    2025 Financial Framework

    Revenue: The Company updated its 2025 projected revenue range to $1.5 to $2.2 billion (vs. consensus of $2.07B), reflecting a $300 million reduction at the high end of the range. This is primarily driven by the timing shift of deliveries of contracted revenue for the U.K. into the first quarter of 2026. For the second half of the year, Moderna expects a revenue split of 40-50% in the third quarter with the balance in the fourth quarter of 2025.

    Cost of Sales: Cost of sales for 2025 is expected to be approximately $1.2 billion.

    Research and Development Expenses: Full-year 2025 research and development expenses are anticipated to be $3.6 to $3.8 billion, lowered from previous expectations of approximately $4.1 billion.

    Selling, General and Administrative Expenses: Selling, general and administrative expenses for 2025 are projected to be approximately $1.1 billion.

    Income Taxes: The Company continues to expect its full-year tax expense to be negligible.

    Capital Expenditures: Capital expenditures for 2025 are expected to be approximately $0.3 billion, lowered from previous expectations of approximately $0.4 billion.

    Cash and Investments: Year-end cash and investments for 2025 are projected to be approximately $6 billion.

data.title: Macau gaming revenue smashes expectations and rises to a new post-pandemic high
data.description:
Macau gross gaming revenue soared 19% year-over-year in May to 22.1 billion patacas ($2.74 billion), according to data published on Sunday by the Gaming Inspection and Coordination Bureau. The GRR mark topped the consensus expectation of analysts for 13% growth and was the highest tally recorded since the pandemic.

Heading into the monthly gaming revenue release, there had been some concern that a typhoon near Macau during the middle of the month may have impacted traffic. However, Macau visitation is estimated to have been about 85% to 90% of the pre-pandemic level. Analysts pointed to the launch of new premium tables, VIP suites, high-profile concerts, and a boost in international visitation as pushing gaming revenue higher in the region.

The strong month in Macau could lead to revised forecasts for the back of the year, including the key Golden Week holiday in October.

For the first seven months of 2025, gross gaming revenue is up 6.5% to 140.9 billion patacas. However, the tally is still only 88% of the pace seen in the pre-pandemic year of 2019.

Macau-related casino stocks: Wynn Macau (OTCPK:WYNMF) (OTCPK:WYNMY), Wynn Resorts (WYNN), Sands China (OTCPK:SCHYY) (OTCPK:SCHYF), Las Vegas Sands (LVS), MGM China (OTCPK:MCHVF), (OTCPK:MCHVY), MGM Resorts (MGM), Galaxy Entertainment (OTCPK:GXYEF), SJM Holdings (OTCPK:SJMHF), (OTCPK:SJMHY), Melco Resorts & Entertainment (NASDAQ:MLCO), and Studio City International (NYSE:MSC).

data.title: Floor & Decor targets $4.66B-$4.75B sales in 2025 while expanding warehouse store openings
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Floor & Decor Holdings, Inc. (FND) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Thomas V. Taylor reported, "our diluted earnings per share increased by 11.5% to $0.58 compared to $0.52 in the same period last year, reaching the high end of our expectations. Sales for the quarter rose by 7.1% to $1.214 billion. Comparable store sales increased by 0.4%, marking the first quarterly increase since the fourth quarter of fiscal 2022." Taylor also highlighted the opening of three new warehouse format stores in Q2, including a return to California, and stated, "We remain on track to open 20 new warehouse format stores in fiscal 2025, primarily across large and midsized existing markets. Looking ahead to fiscal 2026, we currently anticipate opening at least 20 new warehouse format stores."
  • Taylor emphasized tariff mitigation measures, detailing the company's ongoing efforts to negotiate with vendors, diversify sourcing, and maintain a balanced pricing strategy. He noted, "Our direct global sourcing network spans over 240 vendors across 26 countries. In fiscal 2025, we continue to onboard more suppliers factories and products further enhancing our agility and supply chain resilience."
  • President Bradley S. Paulsen stated, "comparable store sales increased 0.4% year-over-year, which was at the midpoint of our expectations and an improvement from the 1.8% decline in the first quarter." Paulsen also mentioned, "customers continue to gravitate towards our better and best tier products where our value proposition is most compelling and our price advantage is most evident."
  • Paulsen reported strong growth in design services, with year-to-date sales significantly outpacing company averages, and noted that pro customer sales "once again outpaced the company's overall growth, accounting for approximately 50% of sales."
  • CFO Bryan H. Langley shared, "gross profit rose by 8.5% compared to the same period last year...approximately 60 basis points improvement in the gross margin rate, which rose to 43.9%, primarily due to lower supply chain costs."

Outlook

  • Fiscal 2025 total sales are expected to be in the range of $4.660 billion to $4.750 billion, or an increase of 5% to 7% from fiscal 2024. The company plans to open 20 new warehouse format stores.
  • Comparable store sales are projected to be down 2% to flat. Average ticket comp is forecast to be up low to mid-single digits, while transaction comp is estimated to be down low to mid-single digits.
  • The gross margin rate is expected to be approximately 43.5% to 43.7%, with "second quarter gross margin rate of 43.9% will represent the high quarter for the year."
  • Adjusted EBITDA is expected to be approximately $520 million to $550 million. Diluted earnings per share guidance is set in the range of $1.75 to $2.

Financial Results

  • Q2 diluted EPS reached $0.58, up 11.5% year-over-year. Sales totaled $1.214 billion, a 7.1% increase.
  • Gross profit increased by 8.5% to $532.6 million, with a gross margin rate of 43.9%.
  • Selling and store operating expenses rose to $376.2 million, up 10.2% year-over-year. General and administrative expenses increased to $69.4 million.
  • Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $150.2 million, a 9.7% increase, with an adjusted EBITDA margin rate of 12.4%.
  • Inventory ended the quarter at $1.2 billion, up 17% year-over-year.
  • Unrestricted liquidity stood at $876.9 million.

Q&A

  • Barath Rao, JPMorgan: "Curious on how much of that was tariff-induced pricing increases versus any sort of trade up to better and best...How are you thinking about price throughout the year?" Taylor responded that "much of the benefit that came in average ticket came from mix. Our best performing department is wood, which carries a higher average ticket...the price changes that we did in the second quarter were not material." Langley added, "our average ticket comp is assumed to be up low single digits to mid-single digits."
  • Simeon Gutman, Morgan Stanley: Asked about comp progression and reactions to 2026 consensus. Taylor replied, "it's a little too early to react to 2026...we'll have to take some price because of tariff...but all that said, it's too -- little too early to react to next year's guide."
  • Michael Lasser, UBS: Inquired about business strategy if current housing market conditions persist. Taylor explained, "if things were to continue to run at this rate...our business should start to grow over the course of time...We've got lots of commercial opportunities...we would continue to invest in our commercial space."
  • Seth Sigman, Barclays: Focused on pricing and market share. Taylor said, "independent channels have taken price earlier, quicker because they had to. We're able -- because the way we turn. This is when the benefit of slow turning inventory is beneficial."
  • Steven Forbes, Guggenheim: Asked about income demographics and the design studio format. Taylor responded, "we are in the middle of revisiting our studio strategy. We're excited about kind of how they're performing...our stores do attract all different income levels."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed a neutral to slightly cautious tone, focusing on pricing, market share, comp progression, and the effects of tariffs and macroeconomic headwinds. Questions were detailed and pressed management on assumptions, but did not indicate alarm or pronounced skepticism.
  • Management's tone in prepared remarks was confident and disciplined, emphasizing mitigation strategies and agility. In Q&A, management maintained composure, regularly referencing proactive planning and adaptability, and using phrases such as "we believe our scale and direct global sourcing model provides a significant competitive advantage."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained steady; analyst questions continued to probe risks but acknowledged improved comps and execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Comparable store sales improved from a 1.8% decline in Q1 to a 0.4% increase in Q2.
  • EPS rose to $0.58 from $0.45 in the previous quarter.
  • Gross margin rate improved to 43.9% versus 43.8%.
  • Management maintained guidance for 20 new store openings in 2025 but reiterated flexibility to adjust based on market conditions.
  • Strategic priorities and risk mitigation around tariffs and sourcing remain consistent, while execution on design services and pro customer growth accelerated.
  • Analysts maintained focus on tariffs, pricing, and macro environment, consistent with Q1.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted the ongoing challenge of tariffs, emphasizing the need for ongoing negotiation, sourcing diversification, and pricing agility.
  • Economic uncertainty, housing affordability, and elevated mortgage rates were cited as continued headwinds.
  • Increased selling and store operating expenses driven by new store additions were noted.
  • Analysts raised concerns about comp progression, pricing flexibility, and the ability to sustain gross margin and profitability in a sluggish housing market.

Final Takeaway

Floor & Decor Holdings, Inc. reported improved financial performance in Q2 2025, with positive comparable store sales, strong EPS growth, and momentum in higher-margin categories and services. The company reaffirmed its disciplined store expansion plan and outlined strategies to manage tariffs, supply chain resilience, and pricing, while acknowledging ongoing economic and housing market headwinds. Management emphasized the company's operational agility and competitive positioning as key strengths for continued market share gains and long-term value creation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
